questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between Programmed I/O and DMA? (2-3 marks)
keyPoints: In Programmed I/O, the CPU is directly involved in transferring every single unit (byte/word) of data.
In DMA, the CPU is only involved at the beginning (to set up the transfer) and the end (upon completion interrupt); the DMA controller handles the actual data transfer.

questions.question: Describe with the aid of a diagram the sequence of operations for a DMA transfer. (5-10 marks)
keyPoints: Initiation: I/O device sends DREQ to DMAC; CPU programs DMAC registers (address, count, control).
Request: DMAC sends BR (Bus Request/HOLD) to the CPU.
Acknowledgment: CPU completes current cycle, sends BG (Bus Grant/HLDA) to DMAC, and releases the buses.
Transfer: DMAC sends DACK to I/O device, places address on bus, and performs the read/write transfer. It increments address and decrements count.
Termination: When count reaches zero, DMAC interrupts the CPU to signal transfer completion. CPU regains control of the buses.
A diagram should show the interaction between CPU, DMAC, I/O Device, and Memory with labeled signals (DREQ, BR, BG, DACK, INTERRUPT).
